5 Question 3

6 Question 4
For the most part, a visual glance indicates that x3 with value 1 has higher y values than x3 with value 0. Although the results do vary in the final curve/outcome, the general shape of these plots are retained.

title: 4  Homework 2
text:
5 Question 1
#pak::pak("thomasgstewart/ds6021c")
library(ds6021b)
library(ds6021c)

set.seed(23583)

d <- external_data(n=1000)

for (fi in c("f1", "f2", "f3", "f4", "f5")) {
    par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
    for (grp in c(0, 1)) {
        idx <- d$x3 == grp
        preds <- get(fi)(d)[idx]
        obs <- d$y[idx]
        plot(preds, obs,
            xlab = "Predicted", ylab = "Observed",
            main = paste0(fi, " (x3 = ", grp, ")"),
            pch = 16, col = "#00000030", asp = 1)
        
        lines(lowess(preds, obs), lwd = 2, col = "blue")
        abline(0, 1, lty = 2, col = "red")
    }
    par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
}

title: 4  Homework 2
text:
7.1 MSE

set.seed(1)
d <- external_data(1000)

mse_vals <- sapply(c("f1","f2","f3","f4","f5"), function(fi) {
  mean((get(fi)(d) - d$y)^2)
})

plot(1:5, mse_vals,
        ylab = "Mean Squared Error",
        main = "Expected Squared Error by Model",
        col = "steelblue",
        ylim = c(0,max(mse_vals)),
        pch = 16,
        cex = 1.5,
        xaxt = "n")
axis(1, 1:5, c("f1","f2","f3","f4","f5"))

title: 4  Homework 2
text:
7.2 MAE

mae_vals <- sapply(c("f1","f2","f3","f4","f5"), function(fi) {
  mean(abs(get(fi)(d) - d$y))
})

plot(1:5, mae_vals,
        ylab = "Mean Squared Error",
        main = "Expected Absolute Error by Model",
        col = "steelblue",
        ylim = c(0,max(mae_vals)),
        pch = 16,
        cex = 1.5,
        xaxt = "n")
axis(1, 1:5, c("f1","f2","f3","f4","f5"))

title: 4  Homework 2
text:
7.3 q90

q90_vals <- sapply(c("f1","f2","f3","f4","f5"), function(fi) {
  quantile(abs(get(fi)(d) - d$y), probs = 0.9)
})

plot(1:5, q90_vals,
        ylab = "90th Quantile",
        main = "Expected 90th Quantile by Model",
        col = "steelblue",
        ylim = c(0,max(q90_vals)),
        pch = 16,
        cex = 1.5,
        xaxt = "n")
axis(1, 1:5, c("f1","f2","f3","f4","f5"))

title: 5  Homework 3
text:
6 Question 1

#pak::pak("thomasgstewart/ds6021d")
library(ds6021d)

set.seed(2343)
v <- external_data(20000)
y_v <- v$y
a_mae <- rep(NA, 100)
e_mae <- rep(NA, 100)
#yhat_v <- f(v)
#mae_v <- mean(abs(yhat_v-y_v))

for(i in 1:100) {
  f <- m[[i]]
  d <- td[[i]]
  y <- d$y
  
  yhat_a <- f(d)
  a_mae[i] <- mean(abs(yhat_a - y))
  
  # External MAE
  yhat_e <- f(v)
  e_mae[i] <- mean(abs(yhat_e - y_v))
}

plot(a_mae, e_mae, 
     main = "apparent vs external MAE", 
     xlab = "apparent MAE", 
     ylab = "external MAE")








The external MAE error is generally higher than the apparent MAE error. The external uses new data to fit the model, while apparent was using the original data.


7 Question 2

optimism <- a_mae - e_mae
plot(optimism, 
     main = "optimism of MAE", 
     xlab = "apparent MAE - external MAE")







hist(optimism, 
     main = "optimism of MAE", 
     xlab = "apparent MAE - external MAE")







mean(optimism)

[1] -0.2455164


The optimism is mostly negative, along with the mean being negative. Based on the plots, the data is mostly symetric on the plot but slightly left skewed on the histogram. This mostly shows that our apparent data was providing more optimistic data than external.


8 Question 3

d <- td[[1]]
n <- nrow(d)
K <- 10

set.seed(125323)
fold_id <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = n))

cv_mae <- rep(NA, K)
for (k in 1:K) {
  train  <- d[fold_id != k, ]
  test   <- d[fold_id == k, ]
  fit    <- lm(y ~ ., data = train)
  yhat   <- predict(fit, newdata = test)
  cv_mae[k] <- mean(abs(yhat - test$y))
}

mean(cv_mae)

[1] 0.9158298




9 Question 4

B <- 200

set.seed(42352)
optimism_b <- rep(NA,B)

fit_a <- lm(y ~ ., data = d)
mae_a <- mean(abs(predict(fit_a) - d$y))

for (b in 1:B) {
  idx    <- sample(1:n, replace = TRUE)
  boot_d <- d[idx, ]

  fit_b     <- lm(y ~ ., data = boot_d)

  yhat_app  <- predict(fit_b, newdata = boot_d)
  mae_app   <- mean(abs(yhat_app  - boot_d$y))

  yhat_orig <- predict(fit_b, newdata = d)
  mae_orig  <- mean(abs(yhat_orig - d$y))

  optimism_b[b] <- mae_app - mae_orig
}

boot_corrected_mae <- mae_a - mean(optimism_b)

cat("apparent MAE:", mae_a, ",","estimated Optimism:", mean(optimism_b), ",","corrected MAE:", boot_corrected_mae)

apparent MAE: 0.6767684 , estimated Optimism: -0.2098636 , corrected MAE: 0.8866319

#mae_v

title: 6  Homework 4
text:
7 Question 1
Based on X1, X2, and X3, the largest possible model would be 4 x 6 x 3 = 72.
The smallest possible model would be a model with all same outcomes, which would be 1.
By calculating the probability that n=30 observations can be found as cells = 72 * (1 - 1/72)^30, which would be

cells <- 72 * (1 - 1/72)^30
cells

[1] 47.3269


This gives us the number of unobserved cells


8 Question 2

x_0 <- 0.45
x_a <- 0.38
x_b <- 0.57
y_a <- 1.92
y_b <- 2.04

w_a <- (x_b - x_0) / (x_b - x_a)
w_a

[1] 0.6315789

w_b <- (x_0 - x_a) / (x_b - x_a)
w_b

[1] 0.3684211

w_a * y_a + w_b * y_b

[1] 1.964211


We can identify that w_a has a higher weight than w_b, which means that the weight would be more weighted towards y_a. By finding which x value is closer, we know which point has a greater influence.


9 Question 3

# x = 0.22, 0.03, 0.11
x_02 = (2.14 + 2.26 + 2.47)/3
x_02

[1] 2.29

# x = .37, .57, .40
x_05 = (1.89 + 1.99 + 2.1)/3
x_05

[1] 1.993333

#x = 0.66, 0.77, 0.73
x_07 = (1.88 + 1.92 + 1.98)/3
x_07

[1] 1.926667




10 Question 4

x <- c(0.22, 0.37, 0.57, 0.66, 0.03, 0.92, 0.77, 0.73, 0.11, 0.40)
y <- c(2.14, 1.89, 1.99, 1.88, 2.26, 2.11, 1.92, 1.98, 2.47, 2.10)

predict_3nn <- function(x_new, x_train, y_train) {
  sapply(x_new, function(xi) {
    dists <- abs(x_train - xi)
    nearest_idx <- order(dists)[1:3]
    mean(y_train[nearest_idx])
  })
}

x_grid <- seq(min(x), max(x), length.out = 500)
y_pred <- predict_3nn(x_grid, x, y)

plot(x, y, pch = 16, col = "steelblue", 
     xlab = "x", ylab = "y", 
     main = "3-Nearest Neighbor Prediction")

lines(x_grid, y_pred, col = "tomato", lwd = 2)










11 Question 5
The “largest model” that has an estimate for every covariate combination can be a poor choice because although it can fit the training data, each estimate is based on its own cell. There is no share of information between cells so in a real case scenario where there may be unknown cells, there is no way to create an estimate for these cells in a largest model.
This ties to information sharing in the way that there needs to be some form of information that is shared between cells to create a rounded out estimate for each cell.
If the largest model had to be the reasonable choice, every cell would need to have some form of an estimate where each cell genuinely stands as its own. Each cell would essentially be independent to the point where the age or blood pressure of someone would not be the same as someone with a year difference.

title: 7  Homework 5
text:
8 Question 1 and 2
set.seed(42)
require(ggplot2)

Loading required package: ggplot2


Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.3

idx_tr <- sample(nrow(abalone), size = floor(0.7 * nrow(abalone)))
train  <- abalone[ idx_tr, ]
test   <- abalone[-idx_tr, ]

X_train_enc <- model.matrix(~ sex + length + diameter + height + 
                              whole_wt + shucked_wt + viscera_wt + shell_wt - 1, 
                            data = train)

X_test_enc  <- model.matrix(~ sex + length + diameter + height + 
                              whole_wt + shucked_wt + viscera_wt + shell_wt - 1, 
                            data = test)

train_mean <- colMeans(X_train_enc)
train_sd   <- apply(X_train_enc, 2, sd)

X_train_sc <- scale(X_train_enc, center = train_mean, scale = train_sd)
X_test_sc  <- scale(X_test_enc, center = train_mean, scale = train_sd)

title: 7  Homework 5
text:
8.1 KNN

require(FNN)

Loading required package: FNN


Warning: package 'FNN' was built under R version 4.5.3

#install.packages("FNN")

knn_fit <- FNN::knn.reg(
  train = X_train_sc, 
  test  = X_test_sc, 
  y     = train$rings, 
  k     = 15 
)

pred_knn <- knn_fit$pred
p_knn <- ggplot(data.frame(actual = test$rings, pred = pred_knn), aes(x = pred, y = actual)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 1, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#C0392B", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  labs(x = "predicted", y = "actual", title = "k-nearest neighbors") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

print(p_knn)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'








mse_knn <- mean((test$rings - pred_knn)^2)
print(paste("knn MSE:", mse_knn))

[1] "knn MSE: 4.97490342016658"

8.3 ORM

require(rms)

Loading required package: rms


Warning: package 'rms' was built under R version 4.5.3

#install.packages("rms")
dd_abalone <- datadist(train)
options(datadist = "dd_abalone")

orm_fit <- orm(rings ~ sex + rcs(length, 3) + rcs(diameter, 3) + 
  rcs(height, 3) + rcs(whole_wt, 3) + rcs(shucked_wt, 3) + 
  rcs(viscera_wt, 3) + rcs(shell_wt, 3), data = train)

mfun <- Mean(orm_fit)
pred_orm <- mfun(predict(orm_fit, newdata = test))

p_orm <- ggplot(data.frame(actual = test$rings, pred = pred_orm), aes(x = pred, y = actual)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 1, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#C0392B", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  labs(x = "predicted", y = "actual", title = "ordinal regression") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

print(p_orm)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

print(paste("orm MSE:", mse_orm))

8.4 LM

lm_fit  <- lm(rings ~ sex + rcs(length, 3) + rcs(diameter, 3) +

title: 8  Homework 6
text:
9 Question 1
In \[Y = X\beta + \epsilon\]
Y is n x 1 outcome vector for the medical insurance charges where n represents every individual counted in the dataset.
X represents the matrix of predictor variables. The first column would be all ones as it would be the intercept. The following columns would be the predictor variables which would be age or bmi and the actual variables that will help identify the research question such as smoker or sex.
B would be a similar structure where the first column B_0 would represent the intercept’s actual value. The remaining Bs would represent the coefficient of the predictors used in X.


10 Question 2
The research question proposes: Does smoking increase expected medical insurance charges, and by how much?
The model answers this question by outputting the predicted medical insurance charges that would be generated for each individual. Each individual will have various of the predictor variables that will be held constant while we can view the indicator variable that shows whether an individual is a smoker or not. This lets us view the differences between the two categories to unerstand the difference in charges.


11 Question 3

library(dplyr)

insurance <- read.csv("insurance.csv")

fit_lm <- lm(charges ~ age + sex + bmi + children + smoker + region, data = insurance)
summary(fit_lm)

Based on the smokeryes variable, the smoking coeffecient is calculated to be 23848.50 and this indicates that smokers’ estimated medical insurance charges are $23,848.50 higher than non-smokers.


12 Question 4

dd <- datadist(insurance)
options(datadist = "dd")

fit_ols <- ols(charges ~ age + sex + bmi + children + smoker + region, data = insurance)
fit_ols

Linear Regression Model

data(Wage)
dd_wage <- datadist(Wage)
options(datadist = "dd_wage")


10 Question 1

fit_lin_lm <- lm(wage ~ age + education + race, data = Wage)
fit_lin_rms <- ols(wage ~ age + education + race, data = Wage)

crPlots(fit_lin_lm, terms = ~ age, 
        main = "component and residual plot",
        col = "blue", pch = 16)







plot(rms::Predict(fit_lin_rms, age), 
     xlab = "age", ylab = "predicted wage",
     main = "pep")








Based on the age and wage relationship, there is a slight non linear relationship.


11 Question 2

fit_spline <- ols(wage ~ rcs(age, 4) + education + race, data = Wage, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

plot(rms::Predict(fit_spline, age), 
     xlab = "age", ylab = "predicted wage",
     main = "pep")








There is a large difference in the implied curve compared to the linear fit. The implied curve has a large increase until around age 40, and then a gradual decrease.


12 Question 3

set.seed(7)
k <- 10
folds <- sample(1:k, nrow(Wage), replace = TRUE)

mse_lin <- numeric(k)
mse_spline <- numeric(k)

for(i in 1:k) {
  train_data <- Wage[folds != i, ]
  test_data  <- Wage[folds == i, ]
  
  m_lin <- lm(wage ~ age + education + race, data = train_data)
  m_spl <- lm(wage ~ rcs(age, 4) + education + race, data = train_data)
  
  pred_lin <- predict(m_lin, newdata = test_data)
  pred_spl <- predict(m_spl, newdata = test_data)
  
  mse_lin[i] <- mean((test_data$wage - pred_lin)^2)
  mse_spline[i] <- mean((test_data$wage - pred_spl)^2)
}

cat("linear model:", mean(mse_lin), "\n")

linear model: 1287.18 

cat("spline model:", mean(mse_spline), "\n")

spline model: 1239.019 


Yes, based on the mse values, the spline model has a lower mean squared error than the linear model.


13 Question 4

fit_lin_cal <- ols(wage ~ age + education + race, data = Wage, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

cal_lin <- calibrate(fit_lin_cal, method = "boot", B = 100)
cal_spline <- calibrate(fit_spline, method = "boot", B = 100)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

plot(cal_lin, main = "linear", 
     xlab = "predicted wage", ylab = "observed wage")


n=3000   Mean absolute error=0.561   Mean squared error=0.57642
0.9 Quantile of absolute error=1.367

plot(cal_spline, main = "spline", 
     xlab = "predicted wage", ylab = "observed wage")









n=3000   Mean absolute error=0.84   Mean squared error=2.02593
0.9 Quantile of absolute error=1.405

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

The spline model seems to have slight deviation at it’s early values and therfore slight systematic miscalibration.


14 Question 5
Based on the following: - Our initial answer to question 1 showed that there was non linear relationship. - The spline model in question 3 had a lower mean squared error than the linear model. - Although the spline model in question 4 had slight miscalibration, it is still well calibrated in terms of fitting the 45 degree line.

y <- riboflavin$y 


11 Question 1

d_full <- data.frame(y = y, x = X)
fit_ols <- lm(y ~ ., data = d_full)

sum(is.na(coef(fit_ols)))

[1] 4018


We get 4018 NA coefficients. This is because there is a smaller sample size compared to the number of predictors. This is found as n-1 coefficients so the remaining coefficients are left as NA after calucating this formula.


12 Question 2

set.seed(7)

cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 0, nfolds = 10)

cv_ridge$lambda.min

[1] 5.934162

cv_ridge$lambda.1se

[1] 30.22944

plot(cv_ridge)








As lambda increases, the mean squared error decreases on our log converted plot.


13 Question 3

fit_ridge <- glmnet(X, y, alpha = 0)

plot(fit_ridge, xvar = "lambda", label = FALSE, 
     main = "Ridge Coefficient Path")








Based on our plot, the coefficients start at various values and slowly converge to 0 as lambda increase. Based on the number of coefficients, we can see that all 4088 coefficients are still retained. This means that no coefficient actually reaches 0.


14 Question 4

set.seed(7)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 1, nfolds = 10)

lasso_coefs <- coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.1se")
features_retained <- sum(lasso_coefs != 0) - 1 
print(features_retained)

[1] 31

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(cv_lasso, main = "Lasso CV Error")
fit_lasso <- glmnet(X, y, alpha = 1)
plot(fit_lasso, xvar = "lambda", main = "Lasso Path")







par(mfrow = c(1, 1))



15 Question 5

set.seed(7)
alphas <- c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)

en_results <- data.frame(
  Alpha = numeric(),
  Lambda_min = numeric(),
  CV_MSE = numeric(),
  Features_Selected = numeric()
)

for (a in alphas) {
  set.seed(7) 
  cv_en <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = a, nfolds = 10)
  
  best_mse <- min(cv_en$cvm)
  best_lambda <- cv_en$lambda.min
  
  en_coefs <- coef(cv_en, s = "lambda.min")
  features <- sum(en_coefs != 0) - 1
  en_results <- rbind(en_results, data.frame(
    Alpha = a,
    Lambda_min = best_lambda,
    CV_MSE = best_mse,
    Features_Selected = features
  ))
}

print(en_results)

  Alpha Lambda_min    CV_MSE Features_Selected
1  0.25 0.15984618 0.2077297                71
2  0.50 0.07992309 0.2108942                55
3  0.75 0.04854863 0.2083931                47


Alpha 0.25 shows the lowest CV-MSE. There are generally more features for all values of alpha compared to the lasso.


16 Question 6

set.seed(7)
fit_pcr <- pcr(y ~ x, data = riboflavin, scale = TRUE, validation = "CV")

validationplot(fit_pcr, val.type = "MSE", main = "PCR: CV MSE")







msep_vals <- MSEP(fit_pcr)$val[1, 1, ]
k_opt <- which.min(msep_vals) - 1
print(k_opt)

23 comps 
      23 

beta_pcr <- coef(fit_pcr, ncomp = k_opt)

pred_1 <- predict(fit_pcr, newdata = riboflavin$x[1, , drop=FALSE], ncomp = k_opt)
print(pred_1)

, , 23 comps

                         y
b_Fbat107PT24.CEL -6.56292


Because each k principal component is used to build the model for the 4088 coeffecients, each gene expression value must be measured/used to calculate the new model.


17 Question 7

c(
  Ridge = min(cv_ridge$cvm),
  Lasso = min(cv_lasso$cvm),
  ElasticNet = min(cv_en$cvm),
  PCR = min(msep_vals)
)

     Ridge      Lasso ElasticNet        PCR 
 0.2318145  0.1984587  0.2083931  0.2221020 


The lasso method achieves the lowest CV-MSE on the riboflavin dataset.
Based on the results, the lasso method works better here. Not only is the CV-MSE lower than the ridge method, it also has fewer selected features that makes it focus on predictors that change the outcome more meaningfully.

library(knitr)

Warning: package 'knitr' was built under R version 4.5.3

d1 <- NHANES |> 
  select(Weight, Age, Height, Race1) |> 
  na.omit() |> 
  as.data.frame()

dd1 <- datadist(d1)
options(datadist = "dd1")
height_knots <- attr(rcs(d1$Height, 4), "parms")


12 Question 1

set.seed(7)
K <- 10
n <- nrow(d1)

fold_id <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = n))
cv_mse_shared <- rep(NA, K)
cv_mse_offset <- rep(NA, K)
cv_mse_int <- rep(NA, K)
cv_mse_sep <- rep(NA, K)

for (k in 1:K) {
  train <- d1[fold_id != k, ]
  test  <- d1[fold_id == k, ]
  
  fit_shared <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots), data = train)
  fit_offset <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots) + Race1, data = train)
  fit_int <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots) * Race1, data = train)
  
  cv_mse_shared[k] <- mean((predict(fit_shared, test) - test$Weight)^2)
  cv_mse_offset[k] <- mean((predict(fit_offset, test) - test$Weight)^2)
  cv_mse_int[k] <- mean((predict(fit_int, test) - test$Weight)^2)
  
  sep_preds <- rep(NA, nrow(test))
  for (r in levels(train$Race1)) {
    train_g <- train[train$Race1 == r, ]
    test_g <- test[test$Race1 == r, ]
    if (nrow(test_g) > 0) {
      fit_sep <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots), data = train_g)
      sep_preds[test$Race1 == r] <- predict(fit_sep, test_g)
    }
  }
  cv_mse_sep[k] <- mean((sep_preds - test$Weight)^2)
}

data.frame(model = c("Fully shared", "Shared shape", 
                     "Full interaction", "Fully separate"),
           CV_MSE = c(mean(cv_mse_shared), mean(cv_mse_offset), 
                      mean(cv_mse_int), mean(cv_mse_sep))) |>
  kable(digits = 3)




model
CV_MSE




Fully shared
330.895


Shared shape
327.016


Full interaction
326.483


Fully separate
326.483





Based on the CV_MSE that we calculated for each race, the Full interaction and the Fully separate models have the lowest CV_MSE.


13 Question 2

d2 <- NHANES |> select(Weight, Height, Age, Race1) |> na.omit() |> as.data.frame()
dd2 <- datadist(d2)
options(datadist = "dd2")

height_knots <- attr(rcs(d2$Height, 4), "parms")
age_knots <- attr(rcs(d2$Age, 4), "parms")


set.seed(7)
K <- 10
n <- nrow(d2)
fold_id <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = n))
cv_mse_add <- rep(NA, K)
cv_mse_int <- rep(NA, K)

for (k in 1:K) {
  train <- d2[fold_id != k, ]
  test <- d2[fold_id == k, ]
  fit_add_k <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots) * rcs(Age, age_knots) + Race1, data = train)
  fit_int_k <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots) * rcs(Age, age_knots) * Race1, data = train)
  cv_mse_add[k] <- mean((predict(fit_add_k, test) - test$Weight)^2)
  cv_mse_int[k] <- mean((predict(fit_int_k, test) - test$Weight)^2)
}

data.frame(model = c("Additive-in-group", "Interactive-in-group"),
           CV_MSE = c(mean(cv_mse_add), mean(cv_mse_int))) |>
  kable(digits = 3)




model
CV_MSE




Additive-in-group
293.061


Interactive-in-group
292.443






fit_surf_int <- ols(Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots) * rcs(Age, age_knots) * Race1, data = d2)

h_g <- seq(min(d2$Height), max(d2$Height), length.out = 40)
a_g <- seq(min(d2$Age), max(d2$Age), length.out = 40)
grid_ha <- expand.grid(Height = h_g, Age = a_g)
rdb <- list(c(0, "steelblue"), c(0.5, "white"), c(1, "firebrick"))

grid_Black <- cbind(grid_ha, Race1 = factor("Black", levels = levels(d2$Race1)))
grid_Hispanic <- cbind(grid_ha, Race1 = factor("Hispanic", levels = levels(d2$Race1)))
grid_Mexican <- cbind(grid_ha, Race1 = factor("Mexican", levels = levels(d2$Race1)))
grid_White <- cbind(grid_ha, Race1 = factor("White", levels = levels(d2$Race1)))
grid_Other <- cbind(grid_ha, Race1 = factor("Other", levels = levels(d2$Race1)))

z_Black    <- matrix(predict(fit_surf_int, grid_Black), nrow = 40)
z_Hispanic <- matrix(predict(fit_surf_int, grid_Hispanic), nrow = 40)
z_Mexican  <- matrix(predict(fit_surf_int, grid_Mexican), nrow = 40)
z_White    <- matrix(predict(fit_surf_int, grid_White), nrow = 40)
z_Other    <- matrix(predict(fit_surf_int, grid_Other), nrow = 40)

make_scene <- function(z_fit, title, scene_id) {
  plot_ly(x = ~h_g, y = ~a_g, z = ~z_fit,
          colorscale = rdb, showscale = FALSE, scene = scene_id) |>
    add_surface(opacity = 0.9) |>
    plotly::layout(annotations = list(text = title, showarrow = FALSE, x = 0.5, y = 1),
                   scene = list(xaxis = list(title = "Height"),
                                yaxis = list(title = "Age"),
                                zaxis = list(title = "Weight")))
}

p_Black <- make_scene(z_Black, "Black: interactive-in-group fit", "scene1")
p_Hispanic <- make_scene(z_Hispanic, "Hispanic: interactive-in-group fit", "scene2")
p_Mexican <- make_scene(z_Mexican, "Mexican: interactive-in-group fit", "scene3")
p_White <- make_scene(z_White, "White: interactive-in-group fit", "scene4")
p_Other <- make_scene(z_Other, "Other: interactive-in-group fit", "scene5")

p_Black




p_Hispanic




p_Mexican




p_White




p_Other





As lambda increases, the mean squared error decreases on our log converted plot.


14 Question 3

d_reg <- d1
contrasts(d_reg$Race1) <- contr.sum(nlevels(d_reg$Race1))
form_reg <- Weight ~ rcs(Height, height_knots) * Race1
X_full <- model.matrix(form_reg, data = d_reg)
y <- d_reg$Weight

term_labels <- attr(terms(form_reg), "term.labels")
assign_vec  <- attr(X_full, "assign")
dev_terms   <- grep("Race1", term_labels)
pf_full     <- ifelse(assign_vec %in% dev_terms, 1, 0)
X_glmnet    <- X_full[, -1]
pf          <- pf_full[-1]


set.seed(7)
cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(X_glmnet, y, alpha = 0, penalty.factor = pf, nfolds = 10)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(X_glmnet, y, alpha = 1, penalty.factor = pf, nfolds = 10)

beta_lasso_min <- coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.min")[-1, 1]
race_levels    <- levels(d_reg$Race1)
race_main_idx  <- which(assign_vec[-1] == dev_terms[1])
race_int_idx   <- which(assign_vec[-1] == dev_terms[2])

dev_mat <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = length(race_levels), ncol = 4,
                  dimnames = list(race_levels, c("main", "height", "height'", "height''")))
for (j in 1:4) {
  cols <- c(race_main_idx[j], race_int_idx[(3 * (j - 1) + 1):(3 * j)])
  dev_mat[j, ] <- beta_lasso_min[cols]
}
dev_mat[5, ] <- -colSums(dev_mat[1:4, , drop = FALSE])
n_nonzero <- rowSums(dev_mat != 0)
status <- ifelse(n_nonzero == 0, "Fully pooled",
          ifelse(n_nonzero == 4, "Not pooled", "Partially pooled"))

data.frame(Race1 = race_levels, n = as.numeric(table(d_reg$Race1)[race_levels]),
           `Nonzero deviation coefficients (of 4)` = n_nonzero, Status = status, check.names = FALSE) |>
  kable(row.names = FALSE)










Race1
n
Nonzero deviation coefficients (of 4)
Status

778
4
Not pooled





Only Other was not pooled at all. The other races were partially pooled. We can see that some of the deviation coefficients were set to 0 for these.


15 Question 4
One-paragraph conclusion: does your dataset support a fully shared, fully separate, or regularized in-between model for the grouping variable you chose? Justify using both predictive evidence (CV-MSE across the ladder and the regularized fit) and interpretive evidence (which groups’ coefficients/curves shrank the most, and whether that tracked group size, distinctiveness of the group’s curve, or something else — be honest if the pattern doesn’t look clean).
Based on our data, the CV-MSE was lowest for the Fully separate and Full interaction models. In our interpretive evidence, we can identify that the Interactiv-in-group model had a lowers MSE. This tells us that the groups have variation among the different races. We showed this by fitting our model on the lasso model and we can see that although all parameters are partially pooled, we can still find distinction between the races. We can identify that the Fully separate or Full interaction model show that the penalty for these models provides the best fit for the data and its parameters.


16 Question 5

X <- model.matrix(~Race1, data = d1)
Z <- model.matrix(~Race1, data = d_reg)

R_inv <- solve(crossprod(Z)) %*% crossprod(Z, X)

R <- round(R_inv, 4)
print(R)

            (Intercept) Race1Hispanic Race1Mexican Race1White Race1Other
(Intercept)           1           0.2          0.2        0.2        0.2
Race11                0          -0.2         -0.2       -0.2       -0.2
Race12                0           0.8         -0.2       -0.2       -0.2
Race13                0          -0.2          0.8       -0.2       -0.2
Race14                0          -0.2         -0.2        0.8       -0.2
